questions.specification:
During the manufacture of fiber-optic cables, sensors collect data on the core diameter in real-time. This data is analyzed using upper and lower boundary limits to distinguish between 'common cause' variation and 'assignable cause' variation, allowing for immediate intervention if the process trends toward a defect.

Which quality management technique is being described in this scenario?
Which of the following is a key component of quality management in commercial production?

A company manufactures Bluetooth-enabled smart water bottles on a high-speed filling line. Each bottle must meet a net fill volume of $600\text{ mL} \pm 5\text{ mL}$, have a leak-free seal, and pass a short connectivity check.

After a rise in complaints, an internal review found three recurring issues:
- Some bottles are underfilled.
- A small number leak after being dropped in shipping.
- A few units fail to pair with the mobile app.

The quality manager wants to strengthen the quality system without slowing production. The team is considering: (1) updating documented procedures and training, (2) adding in-process checks on the line, (3) using statistical monitoring of the filler over time, and (4) sending periodic samples to an independent lab for verification against the specification.

parts.content: Outline two core quality management concepts that could guide the company’s response to these failures.
parts.markscheme:
- Focus on meeting clearly defined requirements/specifications (fitness for purpose) and preventing defects rather than relying only on detection <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Use continuous improvement (e.g., PDCA/Kaizen) to reduce variation and recurring failures over time <marks>1 mark</marks>

_**OR**_

- Customer focus: complaints and critical-to-quality characteristics drive priorities <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Process approach: treat filling, sealing, and pairing as processes that can be measured and improved <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Max 2 marks</note>

parts.content: List two quality tools the team could use to identify the most likely causes of the underfill and leaking issues.
parts.markscheme:
- Pareto chart to prioritize the most frequent defect types/causes <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Cause-and-effect (Ishikawa/fishbone) diagram to structure potential causes (machine, method, material, people, environment, measurement) <marks>1 mark</marks>

_**OR**_

- Check sheet to standardize defect data collection at the line <marks>1 mark</marks>
- 5 Whys to drill down from symptom to root cause <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Max 2 marks</note>

parts.content: Outline how statistical process control could be implemented on the filling line to reduce the risk of underfilled bottles.
parts.markscheme:
- Take regular subgroups/samples of fill volume (e.g., every $n$ minutes or every $k$ bottles) and plot on a control chart (e.g., $\bar{x}$ and $R$) with control limits <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Use out-of-control signals/trends to trigger adjustment/investigation before bottles fall outside the $600\text{ mL} \pm 5\text{ mL}$ specification <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain the differences between quality control, statistical process control, and quality assurance in this scenario, including one concrete example of each.
parts.markscheme:
- Quality control (QC): product-focused inspection/testing to detect nonconforming bottles (e.g., weigh/measure fill volume of finished bottles; leak test pass/fail) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Statistical process control (SPC): process-focused monitoring using statistics to control variation and maintain stability (e.g., control chart of fill volumes to detect drift before defects occur) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Quality assurance (QA): system-focused planned activities that ensure processes are capable and followed (e.g., documented procedures, training, audits, supplier qualification, periodic independent lab verification) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Clear contrast that QC detects defects, SPC controls process variation over time, and QA prevents defects through the management system <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification:
Klean manufactures refillable trigger-spray bottles for hospitals. The bottle is made from recycled PET and must reliably deliver a $1.2\,\text{mL}$ dose per trigger pull (target) to meet cleaning protocol requirements. In the last month, several hospitals reported inconsistent spray output: some bottles dribbled, some produced a weak mist, and a small number leaked during transport.

An internal investigation found multiple potential sources of variation: two suppliers provide the small check-valve, operators adjust the crimping force on the pump head by feel, and the resin moisture level varies by shift. A senior manager proposes “checking every finished bottle before shipping.” The production engineer argues for monitoring the process instead and preventing defects rather than detecting them late.

Klean is deciding how to redesign its quality approach before launching a new higher-volume contract. The team has access to basic metrology tools (calipers, digital scales), a pressure/flow test rig, and production data from the last 10 days. They must choose an approach that satisfies customers, reduces cost of poor quality, and ensures consistent performance at scale.

Figure 1: Klean spray bottle and pump head assembly

![A diagram-style photo of a refillable trigger-spray bottle with the pump head, dip tube, nozzle, and a small internal check-valve highlighted, suggesting where leaks or flow variation could occur.](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/9df271ef-6ddf-4953-9b3b-acf87402bc5c.png)

parts.content: Outline two reasons why Klean should define measurable quality criteria before changing its production approach.
parts.markscheme:
- Clarifies what “fit for purpose” means to the customer and aligns the product with stated needs/requirements (performance, safety, reliability) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Enables objective decisions (pass/fail, conformity) and consistent judgement across shifts/suppliers rather than subjective inspection <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Outline how “cost of poor quality” could be used to justify investment in prevention activities for the spray bottle.
parts.markscheme:
- Identifies external/internal failure costs (returns, complaints, rework, scrap, downtime) created by defects in flow/leak performance <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explains that prevention/appraisal spend can reduce total cost by lowering failure costs over time (cheaper to prevent than fix after shipment) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: List two quality management tools, aside from final inspection, Klean could use to reduce causes of inconsistent spray output.
parts.markscheme:
- Cause-and-effect (Ishikawa/fishbone) diagram to structure potential causes (materials, methods, machines, people, environment, measurement) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Pareto chart to prioritise the most frequent/most costly defect types (e.g., leaks vs low dose) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Check sheet for consistent data collection at the test rig/line <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Process flowchart to locate where variation is introduced <marks>1 mark</marks>
- FMEA to rank risks via severity/occurrence/detection and target controls <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Max 2 marks</note>

parts.content: Explain how statistical process control (SPC) could help Klean determine whether the variation in mL per trigger pull is due to common-cause or special-cause variation.
parts.markscheme:
- States that repeated samples are taken over time from the process (e.g., hourly subgroups) and plotted on a control chart for dose/flow <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explains use of a centre line and control limits (UCL/LCL) to judge stability rather than comparing only to specification limits <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Identifies that points within limits with random pattern suggest common-cause variation (process is stable but may be off-target) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Identifies that points beyond limits or non-random patterns (runs/trends/cycles) indicate special causes to investigate (e.g., supplier change, moisture shift, crimp force adjustment) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Links outcome to action: stable-but-incapable process needs process improvement; unstable process needs removal of special causes and standardisation <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain three differences between Quality Control (QC), Quality Assurance (QA), and Statistical Process Control (SPC) in the context of Klean’s spray bottle production.
parts.markscheme:
- QC: product-focused detection (e.g., inspecting/testing finished bottles for leaks/dose) to separate conforming from nonconforming output <marks>1 mark</marks>
- QC is typically reactive and occurs after or at the end of a step; it does not by itself prevent defects being created <marks>1 mark</marks>
- QA: process/system-focused prevention (procedures, training, audits, documentation, supplier qualification) to ensure quality is built in <marks>1 mark</marks>
- QA sets standards and ensures consistent methods (e.g., defined crimp settings, moisture control plan, calibration schedule) rather than only checking outcomes <marks>1 mark</marks>
- SPC: a statistical method used during production to monitor process behaviour over time and signal loss of control <marks>1 mark</marks>
- SPC differs from QC by focusing on process stability/variation patterns, not just pass/fail against specs <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Integrates with QA by providing evidence for process capability and continuous improvement decisions <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Example linkage: QA defines how sampling/charting is done, SPC monitors the line, QC verifies product at critical points/lot release <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Max 9 marks</note>

questions.specification:
A production facility for medical-grade glass vials uses Statistical Process Control (SPC) to monitor wall thickness. The manufacturing process is regulated by an Upper Control Limit (UCL) of $$2.45 \text{ mm}$$ and a Lower Control Limit (LCL) of $$2.15 \text{ mm}$$.

The table below shows the average wall thickness recorded for four consecutive batches:

| Batch | Average Wall Thickness (mm) |
| :--- | :--- |
| 1 | 2.32 |
| 2 | 2.43 |
| 3 | 2.12 |
| 4 | 2.28 |

At which batch is the process considered "out of control," requiring a suspension of production for recalibration?
